verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: “Jesus saith unto him, Wilt thou be made whole?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: The impotent man answered Him, Yea, Sir, but I have no man, when the water is troubled, to put me into the pool.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: Great is the profit of the divine Scriptures, and all-sufficient is the aid which comes from them.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: And Paul declared this when he said, “Whatsoever things were written aforetime, were written aforetime for our admonition upon whom the ends of the world are come, that we through patience and comfort of the Scriptures might have hope.” ( Rom. xv.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: 4, 1 Cor. x.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: For the divine oracles are a treasury of all manner of medicines, so that whether it be needful to quench pride, to lull desire to sleep, to tread under foot the love of money, to despise pain, to inspire confidence, to gain patience, from them one may find abundant resource.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: For what man of those who struggle with long poverty or who are nailed to a grievous disease, will not, when he reads the passage before us, receive much comfort?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: Since this man who had been paralytic for thirty and eight years, and who saw each year others delivered, and himself bound by his disease, not even so fell back and despaired, though in truth not merely despondency for the past, but also hopelessness for the future, was sufficient to over-strain him.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: Hear now what he says, and learn the greatness of his sufferings.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: For when Christ had said, “Wilt thou be made whole?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: “Yea, Lord,” he saith, “but I have no man, when the water is troubled, to put me into the pool.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: What can be more pitiable than these words?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: What more sad than these circumstances?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: Seest thou a heart crushed through long sickness?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: Seest thou all violence subdued?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: He uttered no blasphemous word, nor such as we hear the many use in reverses, he cursed not his day, he was not angry at the question, nor did he say, “Art Thou come to make a mock and a jest of us, that Thou asketh whether I desire to be made whole?” but replied gently, and with great mildness, “Yea, Lord”; yet he knew not who it was that asked him, nor that He would heal him, but still he mildly relates all the circumstances and asks nothing further, as though he were speaking to a physician, and desired merely to tell the story of his sufferings.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: Perhaps he hoped that Christ might be so far useful to him as to put him into the water, and desired to attract Him by these words.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: What then saith Jesus?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: “Rise, take up thy bed, and walk.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: Now some suppose that this is the man in Matthew who was “lying on a bed” ( Matt. ix.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: 2 ); but it is not so, as is clear in many ways.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: First, from his wanting persons to stand forward for him.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: That man had many to care for and to carry him, this man not a single one; wherefore he said, “I have no man.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: Secondly, from the manner of answering; the other uttered no word, but this man relates his whole case.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: Thirdly, from the season and the time; this man was healed at a feast, and on the Sabbath, that other on a different day.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: The places too were different; one was cured in a house, the other by the pool.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: The manner also of the cure was altered; there Christ said, “Thy sins be forgiven thee,” but here He braced the body first, and then cared for the soul.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: In that case there was remission of sins, (for He saith, “Thy sins be forgiven thee,”) but in this, warning and threats to strengthen the man for the future; “Sin no more, lest a worse thing come unto thee.” ( Ver.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: The charges also of the Jews are different; here they object to Jesus, His working on the Sabbath, there they charge Him with blasphemy.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: Consider now, I pray you, the exceeding wisdom of God.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: He raised not up the man at once, but first maketh him familiar by questioning, making way for the coming faith; nor doth He only raise, but biddeth him “take up his bed,” so as to confirm the miracle that had been wrought, and that none might suppose what was done to be illusion or a piece of acting.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: For he would not, unless his limbs had been firmly and thoroughly compacted, have been able to carry his bed.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: And this Christ often doth, effectually silencing those who would fain be insolent.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: So in the case of the loaves, that no one might assert that the men had been merely satisfied, and that what was done was an illusion, He caused that there should be many relics of the loaves.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: So to the leper that was cleansed He said, “Go, show thyself to the priest” ( Matt. viii.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: 4 ); at once providing most certain proof of the cleansing, and stopping the shameless mouths of those who asserted that He was legislating in opposition to God.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: This also He did in like manner in the case of the wine; for He did not merely show it to them, but also caused it to be borne to the governor of the feast, in order that one who knew nothing of what had been done, by his confession might bear to Him unsuspected testimony; wherefore the Evangelist saith, that the ruler of the feast “knew not whence it was,” thus showing the impartiality of his testimony.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: And in another place, when He raised the dead, He said, “Give ye him to eat”; supplying this proof of a real resurrection, and by these means persuading even the foolish that He was no deceiver, no dealer in illusions, but that He had come for the salvation of the common nature of mankind.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: But why did not Jesus require faith of this man, as He did in the case of others, saying, “Believest thou that I am able to do this?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: It was because the man did not yet clearly know who He was; and it is not before, but after the working of miracles that He is seen so doing.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: For persons who had beheld His power exerted on others would reasonably have this said to them, while of those who had not yet learned who He was, but who were to know afterwards by means of signs, it is after the miracles that faith is required.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: And therefore Matthew doth not introduce Christ as having said this at the beginning of His miracles, but when He had healed many, to the two blind men only.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: Observe however in this way the faith of the paralytic.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: When he had heard, “Take up thy bed and walk,” he did not mock, nor say, “What can this mean?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: An Angel cometh down and troubleth the water, and healeth only one, and dost Thou, a man, by a bare command and word hope to be able to do greater things than Angels?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: This is mere vanity, boasting, mockery.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: But he neither said nor imagined anything like this, but at once he heard and arose, and becoming whole, was not disobedient to Him that gave the command; for immediately he was made whole, and “took up his bed, and walked.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: What followed was even far more admirable.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: That he believed at first, when no one troubled him, was not so marvelous, but that afterwards, when the Jews were full of madness and pressed upon him on all sides, accusing and besieging him and saying, “It is not lawful for thee to take up thy bed,” that then he gave no heed to their madness, but most boldly in the midst of the assembly proclaimed his Benefactor and silenced their shameless tongues, this, I say, was an act of great courage.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: For when the Jews arose against him, and said in a reproachful and insolent manner to him,

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: “It is the Sabbath day, it is not lawful for thee to carry thy bed”; hear what he saith:

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: “He that made me whole, the Same said unto me, Take up thy bed, and walk.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: All but saying, “Ye are silly and mad who bid me not to take Him for my Teacher who has delivered me from a long and grievous malady, and not to obey whatever He may command.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: Had he chosen to act in an unfair manner, he might have spoke differently, as thus, “I do not this of my own will, but at the bidding of another; if this be a matter of blame, blame him who gave the order, and I will set down the bed.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: And he might have concealed the cure, for he well knew that they were vexed not so much at the breaking of the Sabbath, as at the curing of his infirmity.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: Yet he neither concealed this, nor said that, nor asked for pardon, but with loud voice confessed and proclaimed the benefit.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: Thus did the paralytic; but consider how unfairly they acted.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: For they said not, “Who is it that hath made thee whole?” on this point they were silent, but kept on bringing forward the seeming transgression.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: “What man is that which said unto thee, Take up thy bed and walk?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: And he that was healed wist not who it was: for Jesus had conveyed Himself away, a multitude being in that place.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: And why did Jesus conceal Himself?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: First, that while He was absent, the testimony of the man might be unsuspected, for he who now felt himself whole was a credible witness of the benefit.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: And in the next place, that He might not cause the fury of the Jews to be yet more inflamed, for the very sight of one whom they envy is wont to kindle not a small spark in malicious persons.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: On this account He retired, and left the deed by itself to plead its cause among them, that He might not say anything in person respecting Himself, but that they might do so who had been healed, and with them also the accusers.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: Even these last for a while testify to the miracle, for they said not, “Wherefore hast thou commanded these things to be done on the Sabbath day?” but, “Wherefore doest thou these things on the Sabbath day?” not being displeased at the transgression, but envious at the restoration of the paralytic.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: Yet in respect of human labor, what the paralytic did was rather a work, for the other was a saying and a word.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: Here then He commandeth another to break the Sabbath, but elsewhere He doth the same Himself, mixing clay and anointing a man’s eyes ( c.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: 9 ); yet He doth these things not transgressing, but going beyond the Law.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: And on this we shall hereafter speak.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: For He doth not, when accused by the Jews respecting the Sabbath, always defend Himself in the same terms, and this we must carefully observe.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: But let us consider awhile how great an evil is envy, how it disables the eyes of the soul to the endangering his salvation who is possessed by it.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: For as madmen often thrust their swords against their own bodies, so also malicious persons looking only to one thing, the injury of him they envy, care not for their own salvation.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: Men like these are worse than wild beasts; they when wanting food, or having first been provoked by us, arm themselves against us; but these men when they have received kindness, have often repaid their benefactors as though they had wronged them.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: Worse than wild beasts are they, like the devils, or perhaps worse than even those; for they against us indeed have unceasing hostility, but do not plot against those of their own nature, (and so by this Jesus silenced the Jews when they said that He cast out devils by Beelzebub,) but these men neither respect their common nature, nor spare their own selves.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: For before they vex those whom they envy they vex their own souls, filling them with all manner of trouble and despondency, fruitlessly and in vain.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: For wherefore grievest thou, O man, at the prosperity of thy neighbor?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: We ought to grieve at the ills we suffer, not because we see others in good repute.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: Wherefore this sin is stripped of all excuse.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: The fornicator may allege his lust, the thief his poverty, the man-slayer his passion, frigid excuses and unreasonable, still they have these to allege.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: But what reason, tell me, wilt thou name?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: None other at all, but that of intense wickedness.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: If we are commanded to love our enemies, what punishment shall we suffer if we hate our very friends?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: And if he who loveth those that love him will be in no better a state than the heathen, what excuse, what palliation shall he have who injures those that have done him no wrong?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: Hear Paul, what he saith, “Though I give my body to be burned, and have not charity, it profiteth me nothing” ( 1 Cor. xiii.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: 3 ); now it is clear to every one that where envy and malice are, there charity is not.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: This feeling is worse than fornication and adultery, for these go no farther than him who doeth them, but the tyranny of envy hath overturned entire Churches, and hath destroyed the whole world.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: Envy is the mother of murder.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: Through this Cain slew Abel his brother; through this Esau (would have slain) Jacob, and his brethren Joseph, through this the devil all mankind.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: Thou indeed now killest not, but thou dost many things worse than murder, desiring that thy brother may act unseemly, laying snares for him on all sides, paralyzing his labors on the side of virtue, grieving that he pleaseth the Master of the world.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: Yet thou warrest not with thy brother, but with Him whom he serves, Him thou insultest when thou preferest thy glory to His.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: And what is in truth worst of all, is that this sin seems to be an unimportant one, while in fact it is more grievous than any other; for though thou showest mercy and watchest and fastest, thou art more accursed than any if thou enviest thy brother.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: As is clear from this circumstance also.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: A man of the Corinthians was once guilty of adultery, yet he was charged with his sin and soon restored to righteousness; Cain envied Abel; but he was not healed, and although God Himself continually charmed the wound, he became more pained and wave-tossed, and was hurried on to murder.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: Thus this passion is worse than that other, and doth not easily permit itself to be cured except we give heed.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: Let us then by all means tear it up by the roots, considering this, that as we offend God when we waste with envy at other men’s blessings, so when we rejoice with them we are well pleasing to Him, and render ourselves partakers of the good things laid up for the righteous.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: Therefore Paul exhorteth us to “Rejoice with them that do rejoice, and weep with them that weep” ( Rom. xii.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: 15 ), that on either hand we may reap great profit.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: Considering then that even when we labor not, by rejoicing with him that laboreth, we become sharers of his crown, let us cast aside all envy, and implant charity in our souls, that by applauding those of our brethren who are well pleasing unto God, we may obtain both present and future good things, through the grace and lovingkindness of our Lord Jesus Christ, by whom and with whom, to the Father and the Holy Ghost, be glory, now and ever, world without end.
